places.name: Aguas Calientes

visits.text: The train deposits you in Aguas Calientes into another world: the jungle has taken over, slopes are cloaked in dense humid vegetation, and the Urubamba River roars below restaurant windows. Steam rises from the hot springs that gave the village its name. It's loud, damp, hemmed-in—and it's perfect, because it announces something. Collect your Machu Picchu entry ticket if you haven't already (kiosks are available at the ticket office opposite the station), and scout the shuttle queue for tomorrow: the first buses leave at 5:30 am.

activities.text: The natural hot springs (Aguas Calientes owes its name to the thermal waters) are a 10-minute walk from the station. After long days of travel and sightseeing, it's ideal relaxation before the Machu Picchu stage. Turn in early: tomorrow's wake-up is before 5 am.

days_plan.itin_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

map_keywords: Gare de train Aguas Calientes Machu Picchu
Aguas Calientes village Machu Picchu Pueblo

places.name: Arequipa

visits.text: Arriving in Arequipa from Nazca, something shifts in the air—altitude climbs to 7,660 feet (2,335 m), the desert's dry heat gives way to sharper, cooler light. Drop your bags and head straight to Plaza de Armas: everything is white. The buildings, the arcades, the cathedral—all carved from sillar, the volcanic stone quarried from Chachani's flanks, which earned the city its nickname "The White City" and its UNESCO listing in 2000. Look north from the square: El Misti (19,101 ft / 5,822 m) rises above the rooftops with its near-perfect cone. Settle onto a terrace and let the city come to you.

Push on to the San Lázaro neighborhood, half a mile from the Plaza: it's Arequipa's oldest quarter, predating even the city's official founding in 1540. Step into Violín alley or Ripacha alley—passages so narrow shoulders touch the walls—and you'll stumble onto white sillar houses with wrought-iron grilles and flower-filled courtyards visible through half-open doorways. The quarter is nearly deserted on weekdays: wander without a map—it's the only way to truly see it.

The Yanahuara viewpoint, 1.25 miles from downtown, offers Arequipa's most famous panoramic view. Ten sillar arches built in the 19th century, engraved with verses by Arequipeño poets, frame the silhouettes of El Misti (19,101 ft / 5,822 m) and Chachani (19,872 ft / 6,057 m). Go in late afternoon for sunset light over the white city. Admission is free.

Santa Catalina Monastery is Arequipa's most striking attraction: founded September 10, 1579, by Doña María de Guzmán, a wealthy widow who surrendered all her possessions to live there in seclusion, it remained hermetically sealed from the outside world for nearly 400 years. Its doors opened to the public only in 1970. At 215,000 square feet, it's a city within the city: streets named after Spanish towns (Córdoba, Sevilla, Burgos), cloisters with red and orange walls, nuns' cells with individual kitchens, communal laundry. Today, a few dozen nuns still live in the complex's northern wing. Allow at least 2.5 hours—a guide is highly recommended for the first section, then explore freely.

The Museo Santuarios Andinos houses the mummy Juanita, discovered in September 1995 by anthropologist Johan Reinhard and Peruvian guide Miguel Zárate during an expedition on Nevado Ampato (20,702 ft / 6,310 m). This Inca girl aged 12 to 15 was sacrificed as part of the Capacocha ritual, likely to appease the gods after local volcanic eruptions around 1440–1450. Preserved by the cold at over 19,700 feet for 500 years, she's displayed in a refrigerated case at -13°F (-25°C). In 2023, a Polish-Peruvian team reconstructed her face in 3D. The visit lasts about 30 minutes and begins with an introductory film. Photography is forbidden inside.

Take a taxi to the Sabandía countryside, 5 miles southeast: the road descends into an irrigated valley where corn, alfalfa, and olive trees grow, with El Misti as a backdrop—the contrast with Nazca's desert two days ago is striking. At the end of the track, the colonial Sabandía mill dates from the 17th century; restored and still functioning, it grinds grain the old-fashioned way. Cows graze around it, Inca irrigation channels run between the terraces. If you like walking, the path along the Socabaya river offers an hour's peaceful stroll away from tour groups.

Back in town, stop by the San Camilo market for final purchases of local products: Andean aromatic herbs, cheeses, sweet specialties including queso helado (not cheese but ice cream flavored with coconut, cinnamon, and cloves, despite its name).

activities.text: Arequipa is considered Peru's gastronomic capital. In the evening, seek out a traditional picantería in the Yanahuara district to try rocoto relleno (stuffed spicy pepper with meat and eggs), chupe de camarones (river prawn chowder), or adobo (marinated pork stew). The San Camilo market, liveliest in the morning, lets you discover local products at any hour.

In late afternoon, walk up to the Church of the Company of Jesus, whose churrigueresque baroque facade is one of the most ornate in South America. It borders Plaza de Armas on the east side. Step inside to see the interior and its murals.

Late in the day, flight Arequipa → Cusco (~1 hour). Rodriguez Ballon Airport is 5 miles from downtown. Enjoy the volcano views from the window on takeoff—El Misti with its perfect cone is unmistakable.

visits.text: The road from Leymebamba to Cajamarca (about 8 hours) is one of northern Peru's most spectacular. It crosses the Calla Calla pass (3,680m) then the Abra Comullca pass (3,800m) before plunging into the Marañón canyon, one of the rivers that gives birth to the Amazon. The landscapes shift from cloud forests to Andean páramos, from vertiginous cliffs to verdant valleys - plan a day without imposed visits. Arriving in Cajamarca (2,720m), the colonial Plaza de Armas, surrounded by two baroque churches and finely sculpted tuff facades, makes you want to slow down.

The Cuarto del Rescate - the Ransom Room - is where the Conquest's most dramatic moment unfolded. On November 16, 1532, Francisco Pizarro and 168 men ambushed Atahualpa, the last Inca sovereign, in the Plaza de Armas. The Inca, accompanied by 7,000 unarmed followers, was captured and his army massacred. Imprisoned in this 6.70m by 5.18m room, Atahualpa made an unprecedented offer: fill the room with gold up to the height of his raised arm (a red line is still visible on the wall at 2.75m), and twice with silver the adjacent rooms. His subjects brought 84 tons of gold and 164 tons of silver in three months. Pizarro accepted the gold, melted down the offerings, divided the treasure with his men - and had Atahualpa executed on July 26, 1533. The king, to save his body from cremation and be reincarnated according to his beliefs, agreed to convert to Christianity. He was strangled. The room is austere, small, and perhaps that's why it strikes so hard.

Cumbe Mayo, 20 km from Cajamarca (30 min drive), is a pre-Inca site of absolute strangeness. At 3,500m altitude, in a wind-swept pampa landscape, a 9 km aqueduct carved into volcanic rock drains waters from both slopes of the Andes. Built around 1000 BC by the Cajamarca culture - 500 years before the Incas - it's one of South America's oldest hydraulic constructions. The channel, hand-carved into basalt, measures between 30 and 45 cm wide. On the walls, petroglyphs depicting jaguars and four-pointed stars. Around the channel, a forest of rocks eroded into shapes of hooded monks (the frailones) gives the place a timeless atmosphere.

The Baños del Inca, 6 km from Cajamarca center, are thermal springs used since Inca times. Water emerges from the ground at 72°C - the public pools are tempered to bearable levels. Atahualpa himself regularly stayed here (his residence at the springs is partially visible). If you want a break after the day's visits, this is the ideal spot.

activities.text: The road from Leymebamba to Cajamarca is one of this circuit's experiences - not a simple transfer. In the first two hours, you climb toward the Calla Calla pass (3,680m): the road clings to the slopes, switchbacks follow one another, the páramo spreads around you - tall grasses, rocks covered in yellow lichens, sometimes a llama watching you pass. Then the Abra Comullca pass (3,800m) and the descent into the Marañón canyon. This is where the landscape truly shifts: rock walls drop sheer, the river appears far below like a silver thread, and the heat returns suddenly when you were still in the clouds ten minutes earlier. At the canyon bottom, patches of intense green around irrigated villages - oases that the surrounding aridity makes even more striking. Then the climb toward the Cajamarca plateau, alfalfa fields, and Holstein cows signaling you're approaching Peru's most renowned dairy region. Keep your eyes open and your camera battery charged.

Arriving in Cajamarca, climb to the Santa Apolonia hill (15 min walk from center) to see the city and valley in one glance - it's the best angle to understand why the Incas settled here. In the evening, restaurants on Plaza de Armas serve quesillo cajamarquino with cane honey and fruit: a kind of Andean burrata, fresh and surprisingly light.

Cajamarca's cathedral, on the Plaza de Armas, is one of Peru's rare never-completed ones: the two towers were left without bells by the Jesuits to avoid paying royal taxes levied on finished religious buildings. The interior of blond cut stone (local tuff) and gilded baroque altarpieces are worth 30 minutes. The neighboring San Francisco church houses a colonial religious art museum and catacombs.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes

visits.text: This day isn't visited - it's traversed. From Chiclayo, you leave the coast and its ochre desert to begin a gradual ascent that will transform the scenery several times in a few hours. First the dry initial hills, then the Porculla pass (2,145m, the lowest of the Peruvian Andes), and progressively vegetation returning: first scrubland, then trees, then humid forest. Around mid-route, clouds settle in the valleys below you. When you spot the first farms covered in bright green moss, you've changed worlds. Count on 8 to 10 hours of driving depending on conditions - an overnight bus from Chiclayo is a valid alternative if you want to recover a full day in Chachapoyas. Upon arriving, don't force it: the white colonial town and its Plaza de Armas are made for a slow evening stroll, nothing more.

The Karajía sarcophagi are one of northern Peru's most striking images. Nestled in a cliff ledge 200m above ground, six 2.5m tall anthropomorphic sarcophagi have watched the valley for over 500 years. Built around 1460 by the Chachapoyas culture from clay and straw on a wooden frame, painted red and white with geometric motifs, they each contain a high dignitary's mummy introduced in fetal position. Two of them bear a skull placed on the head - a sign of warrior power according to archaeologists. Their nearly inaccessible position was their salvation: looters couldn't reach them. Count on 1.5 hours driving from Chachapoyas to Cruzpata village, then 30 to 45 minutes walking.

The village of Huancas, about twenty minutes from Chachapoyas, is known for its potter artisans who perpetuate a traditional hand technique without a wheel. Pots are still fired in open air. But what really deserves the detour is the lookout overlooking the Sonche canyon: an 800m deep gorge carved by the Sonche River, spectacular in late morning when clouds dissipate.

Kuélap is the Chachapoyas culture's most monumental citadel, perched at 3,000m altitude on a rocky spur dominating the Utcubamba valley. The main platform is 584m long, the limestone walls reach 20m high in places - archaeologists have calculated that the stone volume used to build the enclosure represents three times that of the Pyramid of Khufu. Inside, about 420 circular stone buildings are organized on several levels, with geometric friezes carved at their base. The site was occupied between the 5th and 16th centuries, before being discovered in 1843 by a local judge who had gotten lost. The Incas never succeeded in storming it - a natural defense aided by funnel entrances that allow passage to only one person at a time. To access it: 1 hour drive from Chachapoyas to Nuevo Tingo, then 20 minutes by cable car (Peru's first cable car, opened in 2017). Allow 2 to 3 hours visiting the site. Sleep in Chachapoyas, not at Kuélap.

The Gocta waterfall drops 771m in two leaps (231m + 540m) into a gorge covered with cloud forest - it's one of the world's highest waterfalls according to accepted measurement criteria. Its existence was known to all Cocachimba inhabitants since time immemorial, but legends about a guardian mermaid of the place discouraged approaching it. It wasn't until 2002 that German explorer Stefan Ziemendorff spotted it from the opposite mountain. Only Telésforo Santillán, a villager, agreed to take him there. The official measurement was published in 2006. From Cocachimba (1 hour drive from Chachapoyas), the hike is about 10 km round-trip through the forest and takes 5 hours total. You encounter orchids, toucans, and, if you're lucky, the cock-of-the-rock (gallito de las rocas), Peru's national bird with flaming red plumage. Sleep in Chachapoyas and not at Gocta.

activities.text: If you arrive with energy, Chachapoyas' Plaza de Armas is a good spot for dinner and observing the town. Local restaurants offer cecina (dried smoked pork), juane (spiced rice and meat wrapped in bijao leaf, a typical dish of Peruvian Amazonia), and excellent mountain coffee. The town is small, human-scale, and the inhabitants are renowned for their hospitality.

The Karajía + Huancas combination can be done in a single day from Chachapoyas with private transport or a local agency. Some guides combine both sites and add a stop at the Luya Urco lookout on the return route, with a panoramic view of the Utcubamba valley.

If time permits, the cable car ascent offers spectacular views of the cloud forest and valley below - it's already the show before even entering the citadel. On site, a local guide is highly recommended: the structures without interpretation don't speak for themselves, but with a guide, the story of the Kuélap massacre (over 100 skeletons found in the 1990s, probably victims of tribal reprisals at the time of the Conquest) gives a very different dimension to the visit.

The hike to Gocta can be done autonomously from Cocachimba (well-marked trail) or with a local guide from Chachapoyas - agencies offer early morning shared transport departures. Horses are available in Cocachimba for the steepest sections if you want to save your energy. In the rainy season (December to March), the waterfall is at maximum flow and photos are spectacular, but the path becomes muddy. In the dry season (May to October), flow is reduced but the hike is more pleasant.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes

visits.text: Chiclayo presents itself without frills: no spectacular colonial plaza, no archaeological site in the city center. What makes it worth stopping is the Mercado Modelo and, at the back of its aisles, the Mercado de Brujos. Don't expect superficial exoticism - this is a working market, frequented by locals who come to buy their medicinal herbs and ritual preparations like others do their pharmacy shopping. You'll find dried lizard heads next to flower sachets for luck baths, snake oil vials, clay statuettes representing coastal deities, and San Pedro beans - the hallucinogenic cactus used in shamanic ceremonies still practiced in the region. Some curanderos offer on-site consultations. Walk through slowly, without cameras brandished - the atmosphere is normal and genuine, and stays that way as long as you don't behave like a tourist.

Huaca Rajada, an hour's drive from Chiclayo, is one of the 20th century's most important archaeological discoveries. In 1987, Peruvian archaeologist Walter Alva uncovered the intact tomb of the Lord of Sipán - a high-ranking Moche dignitary buried around 300 AD with 451 objects in gold, silver, gilded copper, turquoise, and lapis lazuli. The tomb was intact: the site had nearly been looted (huaqueros had started digging a few weeks earlier), but Alva arrived in time to secure the location. This discovery is often compared to Tutankhamun's tomb, not for size but for the wealth and exceptional preservation state. The originals are in the Lambayeque museum, but the site itself allows you to understand the excavation context.

The Tumbas Reales de Sipán museum in Lambayeque (~15 min from Chiclayo) opened its doors in 2002 in a red truncated pyramid-shaped building - a direct reference to Moche architecture. This is where the originals are displayed: the Lord's gold headdress, his ear ornaments shaped like golden spiders with human bodies, his pectoral of shells and metal, as well as the skeletons of eight people sacrificed to accompany him to the afterlife. The staging reproduces the tomb's three levels from most recent to oldest. Count at least 2 hours.

Túcume, 33 km north of Chiclayo, is nicknamed the Valley of the Pyramids. The site groups 26 adobe pyramids built by the Lambayeque (Sicán) culture from the 11th century, then successively occupied by the Chimú and Incas. The largest, Huaca Larga, measures 700m long, 280m wide, and 30m high - making it South America's largest adobe structure. From the Cerro Purgatorio hill, the view of the entire complex is striking: you realize the site's true extent. Thor Heyerdahl, the Norwegian navigator author of The Ra Expeditions, led excavations here in the 1990s - the site museum preserves his finds.

activities.text: Chiclayo is known for one of northern Peru's most renowned regional cuisines. The cabrito a la norteña (kid goat simmered with spices), arroz con leche (rice pudding), and especially ceviche of fresh coastal fish should be tasted in one of the many restaurants around the Plaza. The city comes alive in the evening: the plaza's cafés animate and it's an opportunity to observe the daily life of a Peruvian provincial city far from the usual tourist circuits.

If you have energy after Sipán and Túcume, Pimentel beach (~15 km from Chiclayo) is the local seaside spot: a 19th-century wooden pier still standing, a few fresh ceviche restaurants by the sea, and an authentic Peruvian coastal town atmosphere, far from Lima's crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes

visits.text: The road to Chivay climbs steadily from Arequipa. After an hour, ears start ringing. Vegetation disappears. The landscape turns mineral, rust and ochre colored, with tufts of ichu—the highland Andean grass—resisting in the wind. Vicuñas trot along the roadside, indifferent to the bus. This wild camelid, slimmer and faster than a llama, nearly disappeared in the 1960s; there are now over 200,000 in the Salinas y Aguada Blanca reserve we're crossing. At the Patapampa pass (4,910m), the bus stops for a photo. You'll see apachetas—those stone towers that Andeans stack as offerings to mountain spirits. Don't linger too long: at this altitude, headaches arrive within minutes for those not yet acclimatized.

Crossing the Salinas y Aguada Blanca National Reserve, you'll surely spot free-roaming vicuñas from the bus or car. This small wild camelid, cousin to the llama and alpaca, produces one of the world's most precious wools. Pink flamingos also make regular appearances in the high-altitude lagoons.

Early wake-up—the Cruz del Cóndor is best appreciated between 8am and 11am, when the canyon's thermals begin rising and condors take flight. This viewpoint is about 45km west of Chivay, at the canyon's edge. The Colca Canyon reaches 3,270 meters deep at its lowest point—twice as deep as Arizona's Grand Canyon. Condors (wingspan exceeding 3 meters) often glide just meters away, in slow circles on updrafts. Arrive early: the site gets crowded and parking fills after 9am.

On the return route toward Chivay, stop in the Collagua villages of the valley. Yanque, 7km from Chivay, is the most photogenic: its 18th-century baroque church stands on a lovely plaza, and children in traditional costume often dance on the plaza around 7am. Further along, Maca is a quiet village with pre-Inca terraces still under cultivation—the same andenes as 600 years ago still irrigate the fields.

activities.text: Upon arrival in Chivay (3,630m), after the long journey, the best remedy for fatigue and altitude sickness is a plunge into the La Calera hot springs, 3km from the village. Water heated by the Cotallumi's volcanic activity maintains a temperature of about 38°C in several outdoor pools facing the mountains. A must after hours on the road.

If you're physically fit, a 2- to 4-hour hike into the canyon floor is possible from Cabanaconde. The descent to the Sangalle oasis (nicknamed Colca's 'paradise') is well-marked, accessible to walkers of average fitness, and rewarded with vertiginous landscapes. For the less athletic, a walk around Yanque along the Inca irrigation canals, on flat terrain, remains a fine alternative.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

places.name: Cusco

visits.text: The Puno → Cusco tourist bus route with stops is one of the journey's most beautiful road days. First stop: the La Raya pass (4,335m), the watershed divide between the Titicaca basin and the Amazon basin. You step off the bus into dry, sharp cold, smelling of wet earth. In one direction, the flat gray pampa extending toward Titicaca. In the other, the first slopes of the Vilcanota valley, green, descending toward Cusco. Women in colorful aprons sell wool hats and hot chicha from blankets laid on the roadside. Behind them, snow-capped peaks. Get back on the bus before altitude sickness sets in.

Raqchi is one of the least-visited and most surprising Inca sites. The temple of Wiracocha (creator god in Inca cosmology) is the only Inca building with two naves and the only one with interior columns—22 columns in stone and adobe. The central wall originally measured over 12m high. Around it, you still see the circular storehouses that held the Empire's food reserves. Allow 45 minutes on site.

The village of Andahuaylillas houses the 'Sistine Chapel of the Andes': a seemingly modest village church whose interior is entirely covered in 17th-century baroque frescoes of exceptional density and fineness. The Jesuits commissioned local artisans to paint biblical scenes with Andean faces. If you haven't seen Cusco's frescoes, this is where you'll grasp their measure.

The Plaza de Armas has been Cusco's heart since the Inca city's founding. The cathedral dominating it was built starting in 1559 on the foundations of Inca Viracocha's palace, with stones taken from Sacsayhuamán. Its Last Supper painted by a 17th-century Cusqueño artist is famous because Jesus and his apostles feast around a roasted guinea pig (the cuy, an Andean festive dish). Take time to enter.

The Qorikancha (Temple of the Sun) was the Inca Empire's most sacred sanctuary. Its walls were covered with 700 gold sheets torn off by Pizarro in 1533 to pay Atahualpa's ransom. The Spanish then built the Santo Domingo convent directly on its foundations. What you see today is striking: Inca walls in mortarless fitted stone—some have resisted all earthquakes—supporting the colonial church. The contrast is the symbol of all Cusco.

The San Blas quarter, on the hillside above the Plaza de Armas, is the artisans' quarter. Its narrow cobbled streets descend in staircases from the church of the same name. Enter the workshops of wood carvers, ceramicists, and weavers still working by Inca craft rules. It's also the best place in Cusco to find quality handicrafts at reasonable prices.

You climb from downtown Cusco on foot—30 to 40 minutes on a gentle slope—and then the walls appear suddenly, without warning. Three zigzagging terraces of blocks, some 9 meters high, some weighing up to 120 tons. No mortar, no metal, no wheel: the Incas carved these stones and hauled them here with ropes, ramps, and thousands of arms under obligatory labor law. How? We still don't know with certainty. What's certain: the Spanish quarried 80% of the stones to build Cusco below. What remains is enough to take your breath away. The fortress overlooks the entire city, and in clear weather, the view extends to the mountains closing the valley.

Near Sacsayhuamán, Q'enqo is an Inca site carved directly into a natural rock outcrop, with niches, ceremonial channels, and underground passages. It's thought to have been a site for rituals related to ancestor worship. At 9km from Cusco, Tambomachay is an Inca hydraulic site: carved stone channels continuously flow spring water into ceremonial niches. Water was sacred to the Incas, and this site beautifully illustrates that.

Machu Picchu is why hundreds of thousands come to Peru each year—and the site doesn't disappoint. The Inca citadel, built in the 15th century under Inca Pachacútec, was unknown to the Spanish. Hiram Bingham, an American explorer, 'rediscovered' it on July 24, 1911, guided by a local farmer. It was UNESCO-listed in 1983. At 2,430m altitude, the city spreads across a ridge between two peaks—Huayna Picchu and Machu Picchu ('old mountain'). The main circuit crosses the agricultural sectors, royal quarters, Temple of the Sun, Room of Three Windows, and the Intiwatana (astronomical stone). Take your time: the first hours, with mist in the valleys, are the most beautiful. Arrive at opening (6am) for a calmer citadel.

Huayna Picchu option: this steep peak (2,693m) dominating the citadel is climbed in 45 min to 1 hour via a vertiginous trail. The view from the summit, plunging onto Machu Picchu with jungle below, is spectacular. Quota limited to 400 people per day—advance booking required and often sold out months ahead. Montaña Machu Picchu option: slightly easier and less known, but equally panoramic. Same limited quota.

The Museo de Arte Precolombino (MAP), housed in a beautiful 1580 colonial mansion, presents a selection of the country's finest pre-Hispanic pieces—goldwork, ceramics, textiles—in soberly staged rooms. It's small but remarkably high quality, and far less crowded than Lima's Larco. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours.

The Museo Inka, in a 17th-century palace north of the Plaza de Armas, presents a comprehensive collection on Inca civilization: mummies, textiles, ceramics, everyday objects. It's one of Cusco's richest museums for understanding life in the Empire. Artisanas spin and weave in the interior courtyard.

activities.text: Arrival in Cusco in the evening. The former Inca capital, at 3,400m altitude, is one of the continent's most history-dense cities. The first evening is ideal for a relaxed stroll around the Plaza de Armas, without rushing. The illuminated facades of the cathedral and La Compañía convent are spectacular. Restaurants around the plaza serve good versions of ceviche de trucha and lomo saltado.

The San Pedro market, 5 minutes' walk from the Plaza de Armas, is Cusco residents' daily market—not the tourist market, the real one. Fresh fruit juices pressed on-site, medicinal herbs, coca leaves, pork chicharrón, Andean breads. It's lively, aromatic, and perfectly integrated into city life. Go in the morning to see it in full animation.

The afternoon is free to enjoy Cusco at your own pace: final shopping in San Blas, a visit to the Museo de Arte Precolombino (MAP), whose collection is remarkable and premises magnificent (a 1580 colonial mansion), or simply a terrace café on the Plaza de Armas before tomorrow's big departure to the Sacred Valley.

The return is by bus from the site entrance to Aguas Calientes, then by train to Cusco in the afternoon or evening. The return journey takes between 3.5 and 4.5 hours total (bus + train + taxi). Dinner in Cusco in the evening—there are good restaurants on Plaza Regocijo, two steps from the Plaza de Armas.

The last day in Cusco is also a chance to find quality souvenirs: in San Blas, artisans sell handmade pieces—ceramics, textiles, cedar wood carvings, paintings from the Cusqueño school. Hatunrumiyoc street, at the foot of the famous 12-angle stone (an Inca block fitting perfectly with eleven other stones without mortar), deserves a final pass.

map_keywords: Museo de Arte Precolombino MAP Cusco

visits.text: Huacachina is a natural lagoon formed by groundwater that surfaces in the Ica desert—the only true natural oasis in South America according to most geographers. The oasis is surrounded by palm trees and ringed by dunes reaching locally 100 meters in height. The village of 115 residents that developed around the lake was long a spa resort for wealthy Lima families (as early as the 1940s). Walk around the lagoon (about 30 minutes), then climb the crest of the nearest dune before 10am or after 4pm—the heat is intense at midday. Local legend tells of an Inca princess who, fleeing a hunter, wept and created the lake from her tears.

activities.text: The main Huacachina activity: dune buggy followed by sandboarding, in late afternoon. Operators offer outings in four-wheeled buggies (carros areneros) that tear through the dunes with an experienced driver. At stops, you descend slopes on sandboards, either standing for the more skilled or flat on your stomach for beginners. The outing typically ends at sunset from atop a dune. Count on about 2 hours. Bring sunglasses and a scarf: sand flies everywhere.

A visit to a bodega in Ica, 5km from the oasis, is a lovely way to start the day. The Ica region is the birthplace of Peruvian pisco—the city has over 80 artisanal cellars. Operators offer tours with tastings of pisco sour prepared on-site.

days_plan.itin_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

visits.text: About 200 km north of Lima, the Caral archaeological site sprawls across 66 hectares in the arid Supe River valley. It's the oldest city in the Americas: radiocarbon dating places it between 3000 and 1800 BC, contemporary with Egypt's pyramids and the first Mesopotamian cities - and 1,500 years older than other American urban centers. The complex includes six pyramids, a circular amphitheater, ceremonial plazas, and the oldest quipus (knotted cords) ever discovered in Peru. Remarkably, no weapons or fortifications have been found on the site - archaeologists see this as a civilization that governed through religion rather than force. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2009. Plan at least 2 hours for the visit, preferably with a guide.

Huascarán National Park, a UNESCO site, covers more than 340,000 hectares and shelters over 400 mountain lakes. Huascarán itself peaks at 6,768m - Peru's highest summit and South America's third tallest. On the road to Cebollapampa, a stop at the Llanganuco lagoons (Chinancocha and Orconcocha) is practically mandatory: their emerald-green waters at the foot of Huandoy's snow-covered walls set the tone for the day.

86 km east of Huaraz (3h drive through mountain passes), Chavín de Huántar is one of the Andes' oldest and most influential pre-Columbian religious centers. The site functioned as a pilgrimage destination between 1500 and 300 BC - designated UNESCO as early as 1985. What strikes you immediately is the underground architecture: a labyrinth of galleries carved into the thickness of the walls, with ventilation and acoustic ducts so precise that voices carry several meters as if the person were right beside you. At the heart of this maze, in the deepest gallery of the Old Temple, stands the Lanzón: a 4.53m granite monolith representing the principal deity - a hybrid being part human, part feline, with serpent hair, left hand pointing down and right hand raised skyward, as if connecting the underworld to celestial spheres. In 2017, archaeologist John Rick's team discovered in an underground chamber bone tubes used to inhale hallucinogenic substances (tobacco and vilca, confirmed by chemical analysis) - direct proof that Chavín rituals involved altered states of consciousness. Allow 2 to 3 hours on site. The gallery visit may be inadvisable for the claustrophobic.

activities.text: After Caral, continue toward Huaraz (~4h from the Supe valley). The ascent into the Cordillera Blanca is gradual but spectacular: you pass from coastal desert to Andean vegetation, the mountain passes cross above 4,000m, and the first snow-capped peaks appear. In Huaraz itself, the town sits at 3,091m altitude - take time to acclimatize as soon as you arrive. Avoid alcohol and exertion, drink plenty of water, and if you can find it, coca tea.

Laguna 69 is the Cordillera Blanca's most famous trek - and it deserves the reputation. Starting from Cebollapampa (3,900m), it's 14 km round-trip, 700m elevation gain, arriving at 4,600m before a turquoise lake ringed by glaciers. Count on 6 to 7 hours total. The difficulty is moderate to high depending on your altitude adaptation: leave early (agencies in Huaraz organize departures from 5am), bring at least 2.5 liters of water and snacks. If you're not yet acclimatized, postpone this trek to D4 and first do a short outing around Huaraz. Alternative: Pastoruri glacier (5,240m), accessible by car from Huaraz, less physically demanding but visibly receding each year.

On the return road to Huaraz, Lake Querococha (3,980m) deserves a photo stop: its calm waters reflect the surrounding snow-capped peaks. Take advantage of the evening in Huaraz to visit the Áncash Archaeological Museum (Plaza de Armas), which displays Recuay stelae and Chavín ceramics - a good complement to the site visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes
Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes

visits.text: The Mallqui museum, 2.5 km from Leymebamba center (10 min by car), is one of Peru's most astonishing museums - and one of the least visited considering what it contains. In 1997, peasants plowing fields near the Laguna de los Condores stumbled upon a Chachapoyas necropolis. Archaeologists Sonia Guillen and Adriana von Hagen organized an emergency rescue operation: 219 mummies and over 2,000 objects were extracted from the cliff at 10 hours walking from the village. The villagers themselves participated in the excavations, discovering their own history. The museum, inaugurated in June 2000, presents the mummies in a climate-controlled room under glass - same temperature and humidity conditions as the original mausoleum. The bodies, in fetal position, still have their teeth and hair. Among the 2,000 objects: 31 Inca quipus, Chachapoyas ceramics, textiles, and wooden objects. Allow 2 hours.

The Revash funerary site, about 45 minutes from Leymebamba, is a collection of painted mausoleums clinging to a limestone wall. These small 1 or 3-story houses, with walls covered in red ocher, served as tombs for the Chachapoyas elite - only chiefs, priests, and important warriors were buried there. Access from San Bartolo village requires about 1 hour walking. The contrast between the austere cliff and the small painted houses is striking. If you don't have time to go, the Mallqui museum offers reconstructions.

activities.text: Leymebamba itself deserves an hour's stroll: it's a quiet small Andean village, with a tree-lined Plaza de Armas, cobbled streets, and a few restaurants serving local specialties like queso Chachapoyas, a fresh mountain cheese renowned throughout the region. The evening is peaceful and conditions are ideal for resting before the next day's long drive.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Peru Road Trip: Ancient Civilizations and Andes

visits.text: Lima is a city of 11 million people sprawling through the coastal fog facing the Pacific—and the Plaza Mayor is where it all began. Pizarro founded the city right here on January 18, 1535, and the bronze fountain at the center of the square dates from 1650: it's been standing since Louis XIV was a child. Take your time and turn slowly on your heel. On one side, the Government Palace, where the changing of the guard happens daily at noon to the sound of El Cóndor Pasa. Across from it, the 17th-century baroque cathedral, rebuilt multiple times after earthquakes—Francisco Pizarro himself is buried inside. The atmosphere in late afternoon, when the plaza fills with Limeños and pigeons take flight in mass formations, is that of a city that has seen everything for five centuries.

A few steps from the Plaza Mayor, the San Francisco convent is one of the most impressive colonial constructions in Latin America. Work began as early as 1535, but the complex took its current form in the 17th century after the devastating 1655 earthquake. Don't miss the catacombs: an underground labyrinth where approximately 70,000 bodies were interred between the 17th and 19th centuries, with bones arranged in geometric patterns in open wells. The library holds 20,000 volumes including some 15th-century incunabula. The guided tour lasts about 45 minutes, in Spanish or English.

In Miraflores, the Parque del Amor marks the start of the Malecón, the clifftop promenade that runs along the Pacific. The park takes its name from the large mosaic by artist Victor Delfín depicting an embracing couple. Follow the Malecón north or south to enjoy ocean views and watch paragliders launching from the cliff edge. In late afternoon, the light on the water is particularly beautiful.

The Museo Larco, in the Pueblo Libre neighborhood, is probably the finest pre-Columbian art museum in South America. Founded in 1926 by Rafael Larco Hoyle, it's housed in an 18th-century colonial mansion built atop a 7th-century pyramid. Its collection exceeds 45,000 pieces—ceramics, textiles, gold and silver—spanning 4,000 years of history. What sets it apart: its storage facility is open to the public, allowing you to wander among shelves lined with tens of thousands of ceramics. The erotic gallery, dedicated to Mochica pieces related to fertility rituals (100-800 CE), is one of the most famous in the world. Allow at least 2 hours.

In late afternoon, on an ordinary Miraflores street—between a Starbucks and a real estate agency—a 25-meter-high pyramid rises without warning. The Huaca Pucllana has been here since the 5th century, built by the Lima civilization long before the Incas. In the 1940s, real estate developers wanted to demolish it to build apartments—it wasn't excavated and protected until 1981. Today seven pyramids are visible across the six-hectare site, and archaeologists still work continuously among the adobe bricks. Go in the evening if you can (Wednesday to Sunday, 7pm to 10pm): the site is illuminated, the pyramids cut against Lima's dark sky with lit skyscrapers in the background—it's the image of this city in a single frame.
Exploring the Miraflores neighborhood and the Costa Verde promenade
Visit to Museo Larco, famous for its pre-Columbian collections
Exploring the Huaca Pucllana archaeological site
Visit to the Museo de Arte de Lima (MALI)
Discovering the LUM Museum dedicated to memory and tolerance
Final stroll through Barranco and visiting art galleries

activities.text: In the evening, head up to Barranco, Lima's bohemian quarter, just a few minutes by taxi from Miraflores. Cross the Bridge of Sighs (Puente de los Suspiros), a 19th-century wooden bridge that Limeños cross with their eyes closed so their wishes will come true. Then descend the Bajada de los Baños, a colorful street-art-covered alley that tumbles down to the beach, before climbing back up to Barranco's central plaza, where free concerts happen on some evenings. The neighborhood's restaurants and bars stay lively late into the night.

In the morning, before the Larco museum, make a detour through the Surquillo No. 1 market, a stone's throw from Parque Kennedy in Miraflores. It's one of the best places in Lima to see the ingredients of Peruvian cuisine: dozens of potato varieties, multicolored aji peppers, exotic Amazonian fruits. It's lively, photogenic, and far less touristy than the downtown markets.

Last morning in Cusco before the return flight to Lima (~1h20). After three weeks spent climbing passes at 4,900 meters, watching condors from the canyon's edge, setting foot on floating islands, and touching the stones of Machu Picchu—the return to Lima by air takes on another dimension. From the window, the Andes scroll by one more time, white and impossible, before the desert coast appears and the Pacific Ocean closes the journey. If your flight time permits, a final hour in Miraflores or Barranco, one last ceviche facing the sea. Allow at least 1.5 hours to reach Jorge Chávez airport: Lima traffic plays overtime until the very end.

days_plan.itin_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu

places.name: Nazca

visits.text: The Nazca geoglyphs were etched into the desert between 200 BCE and 600 CE by the Nazca culture: they span 190 square miles and include over 800 straight lines, 300 geometric figures, and 70 depictions of animals and plants. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994, they truly reveal themselves only from the air. The small-plane overflight lasts 30 to 35 minutes: the pilot banks the aircraft alternately to each side so every passenger sees the hummingbird, the spider, the monkey, the condor, and the astronaut. Arrive early in the morning—desert winds pick up by midday, and turbulence with them. If you're prone to motion sickness, take preventive medication. Ground alternative: the metal viewing tower along the Pan-American Highway offers glimpses of the tree and the hands from ground level.

The Chauchilla Cemetery, 19 miles from Nazca, is one of Peru's most striking archaeological sites: mummies over 1,000 years old rest in open-air tombs in the desert. Thanks to the extremely dry climate, they're remarkably preserved—hair, teeth, and funerary textiles are still visible. The site was looted before archaeological protection; the mummies have since been returned to their original graves. The visit is not suitable for young children. Arrange an organized excursion from Nazca that includes transport and a guide.

activities.text: Two options for reaching Arequipa. Option 1: overnight bus from Nazca (departs around 8–9 pm, arrives Arequipa around 6 am)—comfortable with major companies, and you save a hotel night. Option 2: day drive the next morning (~355 miles, ~9 hours on the Pan-American South). If you choose the night bus, book a cama seat (reclining sleeper) for a decent night's rest.

days_plan.itin_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

map_keywords: Aéroport de Nazca survol lignes géoglyphes

visits.text: Chinchero, 28km from Cusco at 3,762m altitude, is known for its textiles. Women in traditional costume give demonstrations of natural dyeing and weaving—it's educational and authentic. The colonial church built on Inca foundations deserves a look, as do the surrounding ruins.

Moray is one of the most surprising Inca sites in the Sacred Valley. It's not a fortress or temple: it's an agricultural research center. Concentric circular terraces sink into the earth like a natural amphitheater, creating a 15°C temperature difference between the site's top and bottom. The Incas tested crops from different altitudes and regions here—which partly explains the 2,000 potato varieties cultivated in Peru.

The Maras salt pans are a unique visual spectacle: 3,000 terraced pools fed by a natural salt spring compose a white, ochre, and brown mosaic depending on harvest state. These salt pans have existed since before the Incas and are now cooperatively managed by local families. Annual production is 160 to 200 tons. Stop on the access road for the panoramic view of the entire site.

Pisac has Inca ruins perched on a ridge above the modern village—the ensemble is military, religious, and agricultural at once, and the terraces follow the curves with remarkable precision. The village below holds a lively market daily on the main plaza.

Ollantaytambo is one of the few Inca towns still inhabited: the village streets follow exactly the original Inca layout, with running water channels lining each street. The fortress dominating the town is built in red porphyry on monumental terraces—here Inca Manco Cápac II repelled the conquistadors in 1536, one of the rare Inca military victories against the Spanish.

The Ollantaytambo fortress deserves a thorough morning visit, before groups arrive. The Temple of the Sun, atop the terraces, is unfinished—the Incas were stopped cold by the Spanish conquest. Six 50-ton red porphyry monoliths remain standing on the platform, from a quarry located across the valley. The question of their transport—across the river, then hauled up 250 meters of elevation—remains one of the great enigmas of Inca architecture. Below the fortress, the 'tired stones': blocks that workers supposedly abandoned en route during the conquest.

Pumamarka is a little-known small Inca fortress, about 1 hour's walk from Ollantaytambo. The site is little-visited and often empty—making it one of the most authentic Sacred Valley experiences. Llamas and alpacas graze freely among the ruins. The view of the surrounding mountains is excellent. The trail between Pumamarka and Ollantaytambo follows an ancient Inca irrigation canal for several kilometers—it's a section of the Inca Trail accessible to all, free, and one of the region's finest walks.

activities.text: This Sacred Valley day is ideally done by taxi or private car from Cusco, with a driver who knows the sites. Cusco operators also offer guided day tours. Plan lunch at a restaurant in Urubamba, at the valley bottom, before reaching Ollantaytambo for the night.

In late afternoon, climb to the Pinkuylluna granaries on the hill overlooking Ollantaytambo to the north. These Inca storehouses perched on the cliff served to store the Empire's provisions at altitude (coolness better preserved food). The ascent takes 20 to 30 minutes. The view of the fortress from above is the best in all Ollantaytambo.

days_plan.itin_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

visits.text: The Ballestas Islands excursion is the highlight of the Paracas stop—book it the evening before or the morning of from the El Chaco port. Boats depart between 8am and 10:30am. En route to the islands, the pilot skirts the cliff to show the Candelabra, a 180-meter geoglyph carved into a hillside's sand, whose origin remains debated (Paracas culture, over 2,000 years ago). Then the islands draw closer and the sound show begins before the visual one: thousands of birds—Guanay cormorants, pelicans, boobies—and colonies of South American sea lions occupy every rock. The Humboldt penguin, the only penguin species in Peru, is also visible. You don't disembark on the islands, but the view from the boat is more than sufficient. Bring a windbreaker: the sea breeze is cold even in summer.

In the afternoon, head into the Paracas National Reserve, created in 1975 across 335,000 hectares of desert and coastline. Many travelers stop at the Ballestas Islands and miss what's often the best part of this stop. Stop at Playa Roja, a beach whose sand pulls toward reddish-ochre due to the presence of feldspar—the contrast with the Pacific blue is striking. Continue to the cliffs of La Catedral, a large natural arch that once jutted into the sea and collapsed in 2007 during an earthquake. The landscape—arid desert plunging into the ocean—is one of Peru's most singular.

activities.text: Arriving from Lima (~3.5 hours by road), settle in and take an evening stroll through the El Chaco village. The waterfront is lively in the evening, with several restaurants serving ceviche and fresh fish. It's a chance to eat some of Peru's best ceviche right where the fish comes out of the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

visits.text: The direct Chivay → Puno route (~270km) crosses the altiplano via a little-traveled road, between flamingo-pink high-altitude lagoons, dormant volcanoes, and llama herds. And then, at the turn of a hill, Lake Titicaca appears. At 3,812 meters, the water is a blue that has no name—not Mediterranean blue, nor alpine lake blue: something in between, intense and almost unreal under this high-altitude sky that doesn't filter the light. The lake covers 8,372 km² and extends into Bolivia. In Puno, the idea isn't to chain together visits the first evening—it's to go slowly, drink a coca tea, and watch the lake change color until sunset.

The Uros floating islands are 6km from Puno port, a 30-minute boat ride. These artificial islands—between 60 and 120 depending on sources—are entirely constructed from successive layers of totora reeds that grow in the lake. The technique is ancestral: totora roots form a floating base, onto which are laid dried reed layers 80cm thick, anchored by eucalyptus poles. The lower layers rot and are constantly replaced. On site, residents explain how to build an island, how their community functions—and will offer you a short outing in a balsa, the traditional totora boat. The tourist visit is dense: if you prefer a calmer atmosphere, ask to see the Titinos islands, a less-frequented neighboring community.

Taquile Island, 2 hours by boat from Puno, lives at a rhythm hardly changed in centuries. On this island without cars or electricity (aside from a few solar panels), men have been knitting since age 8—they make the hats and textiles, while women handle weaving. Hat color indicates marital status: white for bachelors, red for married men. This textile art has been UNESCO intangible heritage since 2008. The lake views from the island's terraces, at 3,950m altitude, are among the journey's finest. The lunch served on-site—quinoa soup, grilled trout, herbal tea—is simple and delicious.

Option A - Sillustani: 34km from Puno, this pre-Columbian funerary site overlooks Lake Umayo from a peninsula. The chullpas are cylindrical funerary towers built by the Colla civilization (Aymara people dominated by the Incas in the 15th century), then enriched by the Incas themselves. The most emblematic, La Chullpa del Lagarto, stands 12 meters high with a 7.20m diameter. Remarkable feature: the towers widen toward the top, which intuitively defies gravity—it's thought this served both to showcase the masons' mastery and to reinforce the structure. The openings face east, toward the rising sun. The view of Lake Umayo and its pink flamingos is peaceful and almost dreamlike.

activities.text: In the evening, climb to the Kuntur Wasi mirador (15 minutes on foot from the center), where a condor statue overlooks the city. The lake view is splendid in late day. Downtown Puno is lively in the evening: several restaurants serve local Aymara cuisine, including ceviche de trucha, a lake specialty.

The Uros + Taquile excursion is ideally done as a day trip from Puno port, with a local operator. Leave early (before 8am) to see the Uros in the morning, then navigate to Taquile for lunch and afternoon. Bring good walking shoes as Taquile is explored on foot via sloping trails, and sunscreen—at this altitude, the sun burns even on cloudy days.

Option B - Amantaní Island: the largest Peruvian island on Lake Titicaca (9 km²), less touristy than Taquile, offers homestays with local families. If you have an extra night in Puno, it's the most authentic option: dinner and breakfast with an Aymara family, ascent to the summit temples to watch the sunset over the lake.
Option C - Rest and market: if the previous two days were intense, a morning at Puno's central market followed by a leisurely lunch is amply justified before tomorrow's long road to Cusco.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu
The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu

visits.text: After six hours on the road from the Andes - the coastal desert, the trucks, the heat returning - Trujillo feels like a colonial film set appearing from nowhere. Its Plaza de Armas is one of northern Peru's most carefully maintained: the yellow and white facades gleam under late afternoon light, and you understand upon arrival why the city is nicknamed the "City of Eternal Spring." The cathedral dates from the 17th century; step inside, the frescoes and baroque organ are worth ten minutes. Then circle the adjacent streets to see the carved wooden balconies jutting out over the sidewalks - this is typical Trujillo architecture, unlike anything else in Peru. The Casa de la Emancipación, on the plaza, is the building where the region's independence was proclaimed in 1820 - free entry.

Chan Chan, 5 km from Trujillo on the Huanchaco road, is the largest adobe city in the Americas: at its peak in the 15th century, it sprawled across 20 km² with 9 royal citadels and housed up to 60,000 inhabitants. The Chimú empire that built it from 850 AD controlled the entire northern coast of Peru up to Ecuador, before being conquered by the Incas in 1470. The only fully restored palace open to visitors is Nik An (meaning "the house of the center" in the Muchik language): its 10 to 12m high walls are covered with bas-reliefs depicting fish, otters, birds, and waves - water and the sea were sacred to the Chimú. A ritual pool at the palace center served fertility ceremonies. UNESCO-listed in 1986. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours.

Huaca de la Luna, 8 km south of Trujillo, is the ceremonial center of the Moche culture (100-700 AD). What distinguishes it from all other sites in the region is the preservation state of its frescoes: since 1990, archaeologists have uncovered seven construction levels superimposed over six centuries - each generation built a new temple on top of the old one, sealing the frescoes in adobe. The omnipresent figure is Ai Apaec, "the decapitator god," with protruding fangs and serpent hair, often depicted holding a severed head. On the site's ritual plazas, excavations have uncovered the remains of more than 70 sacrificed warriors. Start with the site museum before the visit - it gives meaning to what you're about to see.

Huanchaco, the fishing village 12 km from Trujillo, deserves a late-day stop. This is where caballitos de totora are still used - 3 to 4m long reed boats that fishermen straddle - a 3,000-year-old practice depicted on Moche ceramics. A handful of fishermen still perpetuate this tradition on the main beach. In 2012, UNESCO inscribed this navigation art on its intangible heritage list.

activities.text: In the evening, Trujillo's historic center comes alive. The marinera, Peru's national dance, was born here: if you're lucky, you'll see a demonstration in one of the plaza's restaurants. Trujillo is also known for its shambar, a thick soup of grains and legumes that local restaurants serve on Mondays. For the curious, the central market (two streets from Plaza de Armas) is worth an evening visit.

Huanchaco is also a good address for lunch or dinner facing the sea: the ceviche is fresh and the promenade restaurants offer fish dishes at prices well below Lima. If you enjoy surfing, regular waves attract a small community of local surfers.
